bibliographic.citation: Eleanor Prescott Hammond, 'Two Tapestry Poems by Lydgate: The Life of St. George and the Falls of Seven Princes,' Englische Studien 43 (1910–1911), 10–26.
bibliographic.title: Two Tapestry Poems by Lydgate: The Life of St. George and the Falls of Seven Princes

dateStatements.label: Issue publication
dateStatements.notes: The volume release statement dates issue 1, pp. 1–160, to the end of December 1910. Later issues completed volume 43 in 1911.

entities.description: Hammond's article includes an introduction on pp. 10–12, an edition of the Legend on pp. 13–21, commentary on pp. 21–23, an eight-line coronation sotelty quotation on p. 23, an edition of Sodein Fal on pp. 23–25, and closing commentary on p. 26.
entities.label: Eleanor Prescott Hammond, Two Tapestry Poems by Lydgate (1910)

rights.basis: First published in 1910, well before the current United States public-domain cutoff for published works.
rights.component: Hammond's 1910 article text and edition

rights.basis: Hammond died in 1933; the ordinary UK literary-work term of life plus seventy years expired at the end of 2003, absent a special exception.
rights.component: Hammond's article text and scholarly contributions

entities.description: A 1430s English parchment codex chiefly containing the Canterbury Tales and ten Lydgate poems. The Legend of St George occupies fols. 200v–204r and is Hammond's base witness for that poem.
entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Bodl. 686

notes: The catalog localizes the language to south Worcestershire and associates the manuscript with the Beauchamp family and Earls of Warwick.
Canterbury Tales occupies fols. 1r–184v and poems by or attributed to Lydgate fols. 184v–216r.

entities.description: A fifteenth-century paper anthology written by John Shirley. It contains the Legend at manuscript pp. 74–81 and Sodein Fal at pp. 359–361. Hammond collates its Legend readings and uses it as the base for Sodein Fal.
entities.label: Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.20

notes: The catalog attributes the writing to John Shirley and identifies Middle English as the principal vernacular language.

access.accessMethod: Use Chetham's official Middle English manuscripts page for the current public item-level summary and digital-image link; use DIMEV for the exact Legend locus.

Email the librarian or archivist using Chetham's official contact page. Request the complete current physical manuscript—including covers, spine, pastedowns, endleaves, blanks, every leaf, and edges where offered—and separately identify the Legend at fols. 193r–198v.
notes: Ask for exact file inventory, reproduction scope, attribution, redistribution, commercial-use, and repository-publication terms before retaining or publishing supplied files.

dateStatements.label: Manuscript production
dateStatements.notes: Chetham's current page supplies only late fifteenth century. The narrower dates come from contemporary manuscript notes reported in the historical Chetham catalog and should be checked against the codex or a current detailed record.
dateStatements.value: Late fifteenth century; copying reportedly begun in 1480 and completed in 1485

entities.description: A late-fifteenth-century manuscript of Lydgate poems, written on vellum and paper. DIMEV record 4108 identifies the fourth Legend of St George witness at fols. 193r–198v; the manuscript was not used by Hammond or MacCracken and belongs to the wider collateral tradition.
entities.label: Chetham's Library MS 6709

physicalDescription.extent: At least 284 folios; exact current collation and furniture not established by the public sources inspected
notes: The historical catalog lists Lydgate's Life of Our Lady, lives of St Cecilia, St Margaret, St George, St Edmund, and St Fremund.
DIMEV maps the Legend exactly to fols. 193r–198v and records the rubric Incipit Vita Sancti Georgii.

access.accessMethod: Follow Chetham's official high-resolution-images link. It currently expands to a LUNA author-results URL that requires reCAPTCHA verification before the image inventory can be inspected.
access.entityLabel: Rylands Medieval Collection digital images of Chetham's Library MS 6709
access.format: Legacy LUNA high-resolution image viewer; exact count and coverage unknown
notes: The verification gate was not bypassed. No local image bundle was created, and neither target overlap nor whole-manuscript completeness is asserted.
A future successful access audit must enumerate the complete published object and prove whether fols. 193r–198v are present before recording a work_portion.

rights.basis: Chetham's official page proves image access but states no item-level license. The gated LUNA inventory and its terms could not be audited, so no redistribution or commercial-use permission is inferred.
rights.component: Rylands Medieval Collection high-resolution images of Chetham's Library MS 6709
notes: Ask Chetham's and the University of Manchester Library for a written credit line, redistribution terms, and commercial/POD permission before retaining or publishing supplied images.

entities.description: Chetham's official page states that MS 6709 was digitized and links to high-resolution images in the Rylands Medieval Collection. The link currently expands to a LUNA author search behind a reCAPTCHA verification gate, so the published image inventory, target-folio overlap, and whole-codex completeness could not be audited without bypassing provider controls.

access.accessMethod: Use the British Library catalog request process. Manuscript consultation in London requires the applicable reader registration and a letter of introduction.

entities.description: A London paper anthology dated 1460–1483 and associated with a lost Shirley manuscript. Sodein Fal occupies current fols. 228r–228v, corresponding to old foliation 254r–v cited by MacCracken.
entities.label: British Library Harley MS 2251

notes: The current catalog records old foliation in the volume; the work's two locators must not be treated as separate copies.

access.accessMethod: The current catalog says legacy digital images are unavailable. Request physical access or reproduction through the catalog's enquiry links.
Use the British Library catalog request process. Manuscript consultation in London requires the applicable reader registration and a letter of introduction.

entities.description: John Stow's mainly autograph paper anthology, copied in 1558 from Shirley-tradition material. MacCracken places Sodein Fal at fols. 169v–170r; the current catalog's adjacent item boundaries are compatible but do not separately label it.
entities.label: British Library Additional MS 29729

entities.description: An English codex dated 1440–1460 containing a London Chronicle and Chaucer's Parliament of Fowls. The third-course sotelty stanza quoted by Hammond appears on fol. 220v within the coronation-banquet account.
entities.label: St John's College Oxford MS 57

notes: Languages are Middle English and Latin; the catalog localizes the scribal dialect to Essex.
Principal contents include Prick of Conscience, a London Chronicle, Parliament of Fowls, and Military Ordinances.

entities.description: Versioned scholarly census records for the Legend of St George (IMEV/NIMEV 2592) and Of the Sodein Fal (IMEV/NIMEV 500). Record 4108 resolves four Legend witnesses including Chetham 6709; record 813 lists the three Sodein witnesses.
entities.label: DIMEV version 1.0 records 4108 and 813
notes: DIMEV's R.3.21 locator in record 4108 is internally malformed; the official Trinity catalog and MacCracken control that witness mapping.
DIMEV record 813 gives Additional 29729 as fols. 169r–169v, while MacCracken gives fols. 169v–170r. Both claims remain attributed rather than silently reconciled.

next_steps: Inspect CME repository history and surviving U-M bulk-distribution documentation.
openQuestions.question: What exact U-M release, download date, and transformation history produced the pinned local CME00085 XML blob?

next_steps: Ask Chetham's or the University of Manchester Library for an inventory or direct authorized object link that does not require bypassing the current verification gate.
If a complete public sequence is supplied, cache the whole manuscript, verify first and last physical surfaces and every target folio, and record exact image/canvas mappings.
If the public object is partial, request a complete authorized reproduction of the current physical manuscript and keep the public selection and commissioned copy as distinct facsimile entities.
notes: The witness identity is resolved by DIMEV; only digital-object coverage, access, and reuse remain unresolved.
openQuestions.question: Does the Rylands Medieval Collection viewer publish a complete cover-to-cover facsimile of Chetham's Library MS 6709, and does it include the Legend on fols. 193r–198v?

next_steps: Review Sponsler's cited textual evidence and scholarship on Stow's Shirley transcripts.
If necessary compare the exact R.3.20 and Additional 29729 readings after obtaining authorized images of the latter.
openQuestions.question: Did John Stow copy the Sodein Fal passage in Additional 29729 directly from surviving Trinity R.3.20 or from another Shirley manuscript?

next_steps: Use the official catalog enquiry or reproduction route and request each whole manuscript rather than only the target folios.
Ask for exact redistribution, attribution, commercial-use, and repository-publication terms before retaining or publishing delivered files.
Map Sodein Fal separately to Harley current fols. 228r–v / old fols. 254r–v and Additional fols. 169v–170r.
notes: No public copy found does not mean no preservation photography exists.
openQuestions.question: Can the British Library supply complete authorized digital facsimiles of Harley 2251 and Additional 29729, including all covers, endleaves, blanks, leaves, and edges where available?

next_steps: Request written permission for any use outside CC BY-NC 4.0, including commercial or profit-making publication.
Keep ignored local research caches separate from distributable repository content until permission is documented.
openQuestions.question: Will Bodleian, St John's College, Trinity, and Chetham's/Manchester authorize repository redistribution or commercial print-on-demand use of the manuscript images?

next_steps: Ask METS or Medieval Institute Publications for terms governing quotation, redistribution, and derivative editions beyond ordinary copyright exceptions.

relations.subjectLabel: Claire Sponsler, John Lydgate: Mummings and Entertainments (2010)

reviewNotes: The encoded cataloging unit is an edited selection, not one medieval work. Part-level scopes prevent the Legend, Sodein Fal, coronation sotelty, and Hammond commentary from inheriting one another's witnesses, dates, form, or rights.
The complete Hammond article occupies printed pp. 10–26 / Internet Archive PDF pp. 18–34. CME00085 begins at printed p. 13 / PDF p. 21 and continues through p. 26 / PDF p. 34.
MacCracken's 1911 manuscript key directly establishes Bodley 686 fols. 200v–204r for the Legend, correcting a provisional range inferred before the edition key was inspected.
DIMEV version 1.0 record 4108 resolves the fourth Legend witness as Chetham's Library MS 6709, fols. 193r–198v. It is a collateral witness and was not an upstream source for Hammond or MacCracken.

Chetham's official page states that MS 6709 was digitized and links high-resolution images, but the link currently reaches a reCAPTCHA-gated LUNA author search. Provider controls were not bypassed; image count, complete physical coverage, target overlap, and terms remain unknown, so no local manuscript cache is claimed.
MacCracken cites Harley 2251 by old foliation 254r–v; the current British Library catalog places the same Sodein Fal item on fol. 228r–v. Both locators belong to one physical witness.
MacCracken places Sodein Fal in Additional 29729 at fols. 169v–170r. The current catalog's adjacent item boundaries are compatible with that locus but do not independently label the poem there.

DIMEV record 813 instead gives Additional 29729 fols. 169r–169v. The manifest retains this conflict and gives MacCracken's direct edition key precedence for the working map pending image comparison or repository confirmation.

Sponsler describes Additional 29729 as Stow's copy of Trinity R.3.20, while the British Library describes the volume more broadly as copied from a lost Shirley manuscript. The copied_from edge is therefore tentative and the description tension remains open.

Hammond identifies the quoted sotelty's codex but gives no folio. Direct inspection of the complete St John's 57 sequence locates the quoted third-course stanza beginning O blessid lady cristis moder dere on fol. 220v, IIIF canvas 452 of 502.
Whole-manuscript caches preserve complete provider sequences rather than cropping to target passages. Each local copy maps the relevant folios, pages, and canvases separately.

Public access and reuse are separate. Bodleian, St John's, and Trinity images carry CC BY-NC 4.0 statements; Chetham/Manchester image terms remain unknown. The project's commercial print-on-demand context requires permission for image redistribution or commercial use even though the medieval texts are public domain.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a pinned copy of University of Michigan CME item CME00085. The U-M encoding represents only printed pp. 13–26 of Eleanor Prescott Hammond's 1910 article: it omits the title and introductory source discussion on pp. 10–12, then retains Hammond's editions of two Lydgate poems, intervening prose with one eight-line coronation-banquet sotelty quotation, and closing commentary. Hammond prints the Legend of St George from Bodleian MS Bodley 686, fols. 200v–204r, collating Trinity R.3.20, pp. 74–81; prints Of the Sodein Fal of Princes in Oure Dayes from Trinity R.3.20, pp. 359–361; and quotes the sotelty stanza from St John's College Oxford MS 57, fol. 220v. DIMEV version 1.0 resolves the Legend's fourth collateral witness as Chetham's Library MS 6709, fols. 193r–198v. Complete public facsimiles of Bodley 686, Trinity R.3.20, Trinity R.3.21, and St John's 57 are cached as whole-manuscript IIIF bundles with exact work maps. Chetham's official page links high-resolution MS 6709 images, but the legacy LUNA inventory is currently behind a verification gate, so its target overlap and whole-codex completeness remain unaudited and uncached. Current British Library records for the collateral Sodein witnesses Harley 2251 and Additional 29729 expose catalog metadata and request routes but no public images. Later DIMEV, MacCracken, and Sponsler evidence clarifies witness loci, attribution, dating, and textual relationships without being projected backward into Hammond's immediate source chain.

catalogSummary.author: John Lydgate; Eleanor Prescott Hammond (commentary)
catalogSummary.date: Sodein Fal: after 1422 and possibly before Lydgate's Fall of Princes; coronation sotelty: 1429; St George poem: possibly 1430; scholarly selection first published December 1910
catalogSummary.region: English cultural origin; London and Westminster are associated civic and courtly contexts, not proven composition places; Hammond's edition was published in Leipzig
catalogSummary.title: Two Tapestry Poems by Lydgate: The Life of St. George and the Falls of Seven Princes (encoded selection)

catalogingSubject.label: Two Tapestry Poems by Lydgate: The Life of St. George and the Falls of Seven Princes (encoded selection)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the University of Michigan CME selection from Eleanor Prescott Hammond's article, not one medieval work. CME00085 encodes printed pp. 13–26: Hammond's edition of Lydgate's 245-line Legend of St George, her intervening prose commentary with an eight-line Henry VI coronation-banquet sotelty stanza quoted from St John's College Oxford MS 57, her edition of the 49-line Of the Sodein Fal of Princes in Oure Dayes, and her closing commentary. It omits the article title and introduction on pp. 10–12. The three medieval verse items and Hammond's modern prose remain separately scoped parts.

contentParts.label: Legend of St George
contentParts.summary: Lydgate's thirty-five-stanza rhyme-royal hagiographic poem, printed by Hammond from Bodleian MS Bodley 686 with Trinity R.3.20 variants.

contentParts.label: Hammond's intervening commentary
contentParts.summary: Modern prose discussing stained halls, tapestry, pageants, sotelties, and visual verse, including one quoted Henry VI coronation-banquet stanza.

contentParts.label: Henry VI coronation-banquet third-course sotelty stanza
notes: Direct visual inspection identifies the quoted stanza beginning O blessid lady cristis moder dere on fol. 220v; Hammond identifies the codex but does not print a folio number.
contentParts.summary: An eight-line ABABBCBC stanza quoted by Hammond from St John's College Oxford MS 57, fol. 220v, as an example of verse accompanying an edible or sculptural banquet display.

contentParts.label: Of the Sodein Fal of Princes in Oure Dayes
contentParts.summary: Seven rhyme-royal stanzas on the falls of seven recent rulers and nobles, transcribed by Hammond from Trinity R.3.20.

contentParts.label: Hammond's closing commentary
contentParts.summary: A short modern prose conclusion comparing Lydgate's treatment of Fortune and signing and dating the article from Chicago.

assertion.notes: Sponsler presents this as a possible commissioning occasion, not a secure composition date.

dateStatements.display: Possibly for the London Armourers' St George feast on 23 April 1430

assertion.notes: The 1422 terminus post quem is firm. Sponsler reports Schirmer's proposal that the poem preceded Fall of Princes but does not establish a secure upper bound.

dateStatements.display: After the death of Charles VI in 1422; possibly before Lydgate's Fall of Princes
1429, for Henry VI's English coronation banquet

assertion.notes: The volume's issue-release statement dates issue 1 to the end of December 1910; later issues completed volume 43 in 1911.

dateStatements.display: End of December 1910 (Englische Studien 43, issue 1); the completed volume is conventionally dated 1910–1911

extent.description: Modeled as five content parts: three medieval verse items and two Hammond prose-commentary parts. The XML contains 43 LG elements and 302 L elements: 35 stanzas/245 lines in the Legend, one stanza/eight lines in the quoted coronation sotelty, and seven stanzas/49 lines in Sodein Fal. It also contains four top-level DIV1 elements because the sotelty excerpt is nested inside the first commentary division.

formStatements.description: The encoded selection combines three Middle English verse items with substantive modern prose commentary.
The Legend and Sodein Fal use rhyme-royal stanzas; the embedded coronation sotelty is an eight-line ABABBCBC stanza.
Hammond's connecting and closing commentary is prose.

scope.description: Latin section headings and rubrics embedded in the Middle English edition

notes: The record deliberately catalogs the encoded scholarly selection and preserves each medieval work or excerpt as a scoped part; it does not merge them into a single medieval tapestry poem.
Dates, places, and genre claims are part-specific. Manuscript production dates and places remain in lineage and are not transferred to the intellectual works.
The XML's 302 line elements include the eight-line sotelty quotation nested inside prose commentary; the intrinsic verse extents remain 245, eight, and 49 lines.
DIMEV version 1.0 resolves the Legend census as four witnesses: Bodley 686, Trinity R.3.20, Trinity R.3.21, and Chetham's Library MS 6709. Chetham is collateral and was not used by Hammond.

next_steps: Review the evidence behind Sponsler's cited 1430 proposal and distinguish documented guild history from inferred commission timing.
openQuestions.question: Was the Legend of St George specifically composed for the London Armourers' feast on 23 April 1430?

next_steps: Review modern dating arguments concerning its relationship to Lydgate's Fall of Princes and the poem's seven historical deaths.
openQuestions.question: What narrower composition date can be established for Of the Sodein Fal of Princes in Oure Dayes?

assertion.notes: The London association is the Armourers' commission and feast context; it does not by itself prove where Lydgate wrote the poem.
This is the coronation-banquet setting, not a proved place of composition.

scope.description: Place of publication of the immediate scholarly article

assertion.notes: Hammond prints the Legend from Bodley 686 and reports variants from Trinity R.3.20.

subjects.label: Fortune and the sudden fall of rulers
Visual and material display of verse

summaries.value: This encoded selection juxtaposes two Lydgate poems designed or interpreted for visual, civic, or performative display. The Legend of St George narrates the dragon episode, conversion, persecution, and martyrdom in thirty-five rhyme-royal stanzas. Of the Sodein Fal presents seven recent rulers or nobles as examples of Fortune's reversals in seven rhyme-royal stanzas. Between them Hammond discusses tapestry, pageantry, wall hangings, and courtly display, quoting one eight-line stanza from the 1429 Henry VI coronation banquet; she closes with a short comparison and publication note.

Hammond's complete article spans printed pp. 10–26, but CME00085 begins at p. 13. The XML therefore preserves the two editions and later commentary while omitting the article title and introductory source discussion on pp. 10–12.

assertion.notes: The parenthetical qualifier makes the U-M boundary explicit: the XML starts on article p. 13 and omits the title and introduction on pp. 10–12.

notes: This title is borne by Hammond's complete source article, not by the pp. 13–26 selection as independently published matter.

notes: Hammond's running heading says Life of St. George; MacCracken and Sponsler use Legend of St. George.
Hammond also calls the poem The Falls of Seven Princes.

work.summary: This encoded selection juxtaposes two Lydgate poems designed or interpreted for visual, civic, or performative display. The Legend of St George narrates the dragon episode, conversion, persecution, and martyrdom in thirty-five rhyme-royal stanzas. Of the Sodein Fal presents seven recent rulers or nobles as examples of Fortune's reversals in seven rhyme-royal stanzas. Between them Hammond discusses tapestry, pageantry, wall hangings, and courtly display, quoting one eight-line stanza from the 1429 Henry VI coronation banquet; she closes with a short comparison and publication note.
